past_medical_history: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed at age 2 for post-meningitis hydrocephalus, revised once at age 14
Neonatal pneumococcal meningitis at 3 weeks of life with prolonged NICU stay
Remote history of febrile seizures in childhood, no seizures since age 6
Mild residual left-sided spastic hemiparesis from neonatal meningitis, functionally independent
Chronic tension-type headaches managed with over-the-counter analgesics

medications.frequency: as needed for headache, approximately 3 times per week

family_history: No family history of immunodeficiency or neurological disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional beer on weekends, 2-3 per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and 5-year-old daughter in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Walks regularly, limited vigorous activity due to mild left-sided spasticity
social_history.travel: No recent travel outside the country

neurological_exam.mental_status: Somnolent, arousable to voice but falls asleep when unstimulated. Oriented to self only; unable to state date, location, or situation. Speech is slow but coherent when aroused. Unable to perform serial 7s. GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. Marked photophobia with grimacing and eye closure to penlight. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild left lower facial weakness (chronic, per wife). Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right-sided extremities 4+/5 throughout. Left upper and lower extremities 4-/5, with increased tone consistent with chronic spasticity (baseline per history). No acute asymmetry beyond known deficit. No pronator drift on right; unable to assess fully on left due to baseline spasticity.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws symmetrically to painful stimuli in all extremities. Formal sensory testing limited by encephalopathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Left biceps 3+, triceps 3+, patellar 3+, Achilles 3+ (chronically brisk per baseline). Plantar response flexor on right, extensor on left (chronic, per prior documentation).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to reliably assess due to altered mental status. Gross reaching appears intact on right side.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient too somnolent and unsteady to stand safely.
neurological_exam.additional: Severe nuchal rigidity present. Kernig sign positive bilaterally with resistance and pain at approximately 130 degrees. Brudzinski sign positive with involuntary hip and knee flexion on passive neck flexion. VP shunt valve palpable in right parietal region, no erythema or tenderness along shunt tract. No skin rash or petechiae. Tympanic membranes normal bilaterally.

patient.chief_complaint: Severe headache, high fever, and progressive confusion over 18 hours
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Ricardo Fuentes is a 38-year-old right-handed man who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife found him increasingly confused and febrile at home. He has a significant past medical history of neonatal pneumococcal meningitis complicated by hydrocephalus requiring ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement in infancy, with a single shunt revision during adolescence. He has a residual mild left-sided spastic hemiparesis but is functionally independent in all activities of daily living and works full-time as a warehouse logistics supervisor. Yesterday morning, approximately 18 hours before presentation, the patient developed a frontal headache that he initially attributed to his chronic tension headaches. He took his usual ibuprofen without relief. By the afternoon, his wife noticed he felt warm and took his temperature, recording 38.6 degrees Celsius. He complained of worsening neck stiffness that he described as different from his usual headaches, specifically localized to the posterior neck and exacerbated by bending forward. He took acetaminophen and went to lie down in a dark room, complaining that the living room lights were causing intense pain. Overnight, his condition deteriorated rapidly. His wife heard him moaning and found him curled on his side, drenched in sweat, and mumbling incoherently. He was unable to recognize their daughter and became agitated when she tried to sit him up. He vomited three times between midnight and early morning. By 05:00 this morning, he was barely responsive to verbal commands. His wife called 911 when she was unable to rouse him from bed. Paramedics recorded a temperature of 39.9 degrees Celsius en route and noted significant neck rigidity during assessment. His wife reports no recent sick contacts with meningitis, no recent dental procedures, and no trauma to the head or shunt site. She notes he had a mild upper respiratory infection with nasal congestion approximately ten days ago that resolved on its own. She is unsure of his pneumococcal vaccination status but believes he received childhood vaccinations. There has been no seizure activity witnessed, no new rashes, and no recent changes in shunt function.

findings.location: Diffuse bilateral cerebral convexities, sylvian fissures, and basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Avid, diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement along bilateral convexities and basal cisterns, most prominent over the vertex and along the sylvian fissures
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal within bilateral cerebral sulci, indicating proteinaceous or purulent subarachnoid material
signal_characteristics.DWI: Multifocal areas of restricted diffusion within cortical sulci bilaterally, consistent with purulent exudate

findings.mass_effect: Mild diffuse cerebral edema with subtle sulcal effacement. No midline shift. No uncal or tonsillar herniation.
findings.borders: Diffuse bilateral involvement without focal parenchymal lesion

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense, consistent with chronic tissue loss
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense rim with central CSF-signal cavity

findings.mass_effect: None; associated mild ex-vacuo dilatation of the left lateral ventricle

findings.location: Right parietal approach with catheter tip in the right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle

signal_characteristics.T1: Linear signal void consistent with shunt catheter

additional_observations: VP shunt catheter in appropriate position with tip in the right frontal horn; no evidence of catheter disconnection or migration
Ventricles are normal in size and symmetric, no hydrocephalus
No subdural empyema, epidural abscess, or intraparenchymal abscess identified
No evidence of dural venous sinus thrombosis on post-contrast sequences
mri.impression: VP shunt catheter in satisfactory position without evidence of malfunction or associated infection. No acute complication such as abscess, empyema, or venous thrombosis identified. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: pneumococcal meningitis VP shunt recurrence risk adults

results.title: Recurrent bacterial meningitis in adults with CSF shunts: risk factors and management
results.summary: Patients with VP shunts have a 5-10% risk of shunt-related CNS infection over the lifetime of the device. Recurrent community-acquired meningitis in shunted patients may occur via hematogenous seeding or contiguous spread rather than direct shunt colonization.

results.title: Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis in adults: clinical features and outcomes in the post-vaccination era
results.summary: Pneumococcal meningitis remains the most common and most severe form of bacterial meningitis in adults. Mortality ranges from 15-30%. Patients with prior CNS insults, CSF leaks, or shunts are at increased risk. Dexamethasone adjunctive therapy before or with first dose of antibiotics reduces mortality and neurological sequelae.

results.title: Pneumococcal vaccination in high-risk adults: ACIP recommendations update
results.summary: Adults with CSF leaks or cochlear implants are considered at high risk for pneumococcal disease. Vaccination with PCV20 or PCV15 followed by PPSV23 is recommended. Patients with history of invasive pneumococcal disease should be vaccinated regardless of prior vaccination status.

output.summary: Literature review confirms that patients with VP shunts are at elevated risk for both shunt-related CNS infections and community-acquired bacterial meningitis. Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in adults, with significant mortality (15-30%). Adjunctive dexamethasone is recommended. Post-recovery, pneumococcal vaccination is strongly recommended, especially in patients with CSF-communicating devices.

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days as adjunctive therapy for bacterial meningitis
interactions: Ibuprofen + Dexamethasone: Increased risk of GI bleeding and peptic ulceration with concurrent NSAID and corticosteroid use. Discontinue ibuprofen during dexamethasone course.
warnings: Dexamethasone may mask fever, complicating clinical monitoring of treatment response
Monitor blood glucose closely as corticosteroids cause hyperglycemia
Dexamethasone should be administered before or with the first dose of antibiotics for maximum benefit; benefit is reduced if given after antibiotics have been started
alternatives: No alternative corticosteroid is recommended; dexamethasone is the standard adjunctive therapy for bacterial meningitis based on IDSA guidelines

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Shunt tap CSF WBC 8 cells/uL cells/uL (H), Shunt tap CSF protein 52 mg/dL mg/dL (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Shunt CSF WBC mildly elevated at 8 cells/uL (reactive)
Shunt CSF protein mildly elevated at 52 mg/dL (reactive)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal meningitis)

differential.key_distinguishing: Would show lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, lower protein, and less toxic-appearing patient; CSF gram stain and culture negative for bacteria
Shunt infection typically shows abnormal ventricular CSF with organisms on shunt tap; this patient has clear ventricular CSF with negative gram stain and culture
Would show temporal lobe involvement on MRI, seizures, personality changes, and lymphocytic CSF; PCR panel negative for HSV
Thunderclap headache, xanthochromia, RBCs in CSF, CT showing blood in subarachnoid space; not consistent with current presentation
Subacute course over weeks, basilar meningeal enhancement, lymphocytic pleocytosis, and very low glucose but less acute presentation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain non-contrast CT head to rule out mass lesion, hydrocephalus, or hemorrhage before lumbar puncture, especially given VP shunt history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute hydrocephalus, hemorrhage, or mass lesion; chronic encephalomalacia; shunt in place

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture, and lactate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF with elevated opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein, very low glucose, gram-positive diplococci on stain

optimal_actions.action: Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gram-positive diplococci growing in blood cultures, consistent with pneumococcal bacteremia

optimal_actions.action: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h) and adjunctive dexamethasone immediately, do not delay for diagnostic workup
Interpret CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, and coagulation studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, elevated CRP and procalcitonin, hyponatremia, elevated lactate

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to evaluate extent of meningeal disease and rule out complications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement, sulcal restricted diffusion consistent with purulent exudate, no abscess or empyema

optimal_actions.action: Order CSF meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel for rapid pathogen identification
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Streptococcus pneumoniae detected; all other targets negative

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for VP shunt evaluation and possible shunt tap to rule out shunt infection
Check drug interactions for dexamethasone and current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Interaction between ibuprofen and dexamethasone (increased GI bleed risk); hold ibuprofen

optimal_actions.action: Repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours to assess treatment response if clinically indicated
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improving CSF parameters with decreasing WBC, improving glucose, and negative gram stain

critical_actions: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone plus vancomycin) within 1 hour of presentation; do not delay for imaging or LP
Administer adjunctive IV dexamethasone before or with the first dose of antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for gram stain, culture, cell count, protein, and glucose
Obtain blood cultures before initiating antibiotic therapy
Obtain CT head before lumbar puncture given VP shunt and altered mental status to rule out contraindications
Monitor for complications: seizures, cerebral edema, SIADH, DIC, and hearing loss
contraindicated_actions: Delay antibiotics to await imaging or LP results
Perform lumbar puncture without prior CT imaging in a patient with VP shunt and altered mental status
Discontinue or remove VP shunt without evidence of shunt infection
Administer high-dose corticosteroids concurrently with NSAIDs without gastroprotection
Administer aminoglycosides as first-line therapy due to poor CNS penetration
key_reasoning_points: Classic triad of fever, neck stiffness, and altered mental status in a patient with VP shunt history should raise immediate suspicion for bacterial meningitis
History of neonatal pneumococcal meningitis places this patient at increased risk for recurrent meningitis, potentially related to anatomical predisposition or immunological factors
VP shunt presence necessitates CT head before LP and neurosurgical consultation to differentiate community-acquired meningitis from shunt infection

CSF findings of turbid fluid, neutrophilic pleocytosis (3200 WBC, 94% neutrophils), very low glucose (12 mg/dL, ratio 0.09), elevated protein (385 mg/dL), and gram-positive diplococci are diagnostic of pneumococcal meningitis
Adjunctive dexamethasone must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and neurological sequelae, particularly hearing loss, in pneumococcal meningitis
The chronic left frontoparietal encephalomalacia on MRI is a sequela of the patient's neonatal meningitis and should not be confused with an acute finding
Shunt tap showing clear CSF with negative gram stain and culture effectively rules out shunt infection as the source, confirming community-acquired meningitis
Hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) should raise concern for SIADH, a common complication of bacterial meningitis requiring fluid restriction and monitoring

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic meningeal signs, high fever, and altered mental status. VP shunt history adds a clinically relevant layer requiring differentiation from shunt infection, but CSF and imaging findings are unambiguous for community-acquired pneumococcal meningitis.
